question: Explain the purpose of Java packages and describe two key benefits they provide for software development.
expectedPoints: Packages are used to organize related classes and interfaces into namespaces, preventing naming conflicts.
They provide access control; classes within the same package have access to each other's package-private (default) members.
Packages improve maintainability and reusability by logically grouping code and making it easier to locate related functionality.

question: Describe the effect of the 'protected' access modifier on a member variable. Specifically, explain from where a protected member can be accessed.
expectedPoints: A protected member is accessible within its own class.
It is accessible within any class that is in the same package.
It is accessible within a subclass, even if the subclass is in a different package.

question: Compare and contrast the four member access modifiers in Java (public, protected, default (package-private), and private). For each, detail the level of access they permit and provide a typical use-case scenario.
expectedPoints: Private: Accessible only within the own class. Used for internal implementation details that should be hidden (encapsulation).
Default (Package-Private): Accessible only within the same package. Used for members that should be shared between classes in a package but hidden from the outside.
Protected: Accessible within the same package and by subclasses (even in different packages). Used for members intended to be inherited and modified by subclasses.
Public: Accessible from any other class. Used for the official API that defines how other code should interact with the class.
